frames.caption: Initialize the converging interval: left = 0, right = 4. Walk the two pointers inward.
frames.note: Converging two pointers: one index starts at each end of the array and they walk toward each other. The whole job is to mirror the array, so pairing the outermost characters (s[L] with s[R]) and working inward visits every element exactly once. Because we mutate the input array in place, this is O(n) time and O(1) extra space — no second buffer is allocated.

frames.caption: left (0) < right (4): swap s[0]='h' with s[4]='o'. Then step inward.
frames.note: The loop guard left < right is still true, so s[L] and s[R] are a genuine pair on opposite sides of the center. The tuple swap (s[left], s[right]) = (s[right], s[left]) exchanges them with no temp variable, then left++ and right-- step both pointers one slot inward. Each iteration places two characters in their final reversed positions, which is why the array is reversed after only n/2 swaps.

frames.caption: left (1) < right (3): swap s[1]='e' with s[3]='l'. Then step inward.
frames.note: The loop guard left < right is still true, so s[L] and s[R] are a genuine pair on opposite sides of the center. The tuple swap (s[left], s[right]) = (s[right], s[left]) exchanges them with no temp variable, then left++ and right-- step both pointers one slot inward. Each iteration places two characters in their final reversed positions, which is why the array is reversed after only n/2 swaps.

frames.caption: left (2) is no longer < right (2) — they met. The middle character stays put; stop.
frames.note: L and R have met on the same index (odd-length array), so the guard left < right is now false and the loop stops. The single middle character is already in its correct mirrored position — its reverse is itself — so it is left untouched. Stopping the instant the pointers meet (rather than left <= right) avoids re-swapping the pairs we already fixed.

frames.caption: The array is reversed in place: ['o','l','l','e','h'].
frames.note: Every pair from the ends inward has been swapped, so the array now reads back-to-front. Notice the work: 5 characters took just 2 swaps. The converging pattern reverses in place with O(1) space — the headline reason it beats building a new reversed copy.
